meta.title: Surco — Get your DJ tracks ready in seconds
meta.description: Surco automates DJ track prep: convert to lossless AIFF, WAV or FLAC (or a lighter MP3), tag from Discogs and send to Apple Music in seconds — and catch re-encoded MP3s disguised as lossless.
meta.ogTitle: Surco — Get your DJ tracks ready in seconds
meta.ogDescription: Less prepping, more playing: convert to lossless AIFF, WAV or FLAC (or a lighter MP3), tag from Discogs and organize in seconds. And catch MP3s disguised as lossless.
meta.jsonLdDescription: Audio track organizer for DJs: convert to lossless AIFF, WAV or FLAC, tag from Discogs, send to Apple Music and analyze the spectrum to detect re-encoded MP3s.

betaNote: Surco is in beta. No account, no subscription.

hero.ledeShort: Convert, tag, check the quality and file into Apple Music: what used to take four or five apps, Surco does in one. From minutes per track to a couple of seconds.

replaces.title: The work of five apps, in Surco
replaces.lede: Surco reinvents nothing: it gathers into a single window what you used to do jumping across five programs.

analysis.title: The spectrum doesn't lie
analysis.lede: Some "lossless" files are just low-quality MP3s dressed up to look hi-fi — and they sound worse on a big system. Surco spots the missing highs and flags the track before it reaches your set.
analysis.suspectCaptionPost: — the highs are gone. A fake.

features.title: The whole flow, in one app

groups.title: Convert
items: AIFF, WAV, FLAC or MP3, any to any
Already in that format? Copies it as-is, instantly
Match every track to the same loudness or peak (optional)
Re-export without touching Traktor's cue points or beatgrid

groups.title: Tag
groups.replaces: instead of Discogs in the browser and a tag editor
items: Fill artist, album, year, label, BPM and key from Discogs in one click
Match a whole release by length, even when files share a name
Bulk-edit and find-and-replace (text or regex) across the queue
Pull the cover from Discogs or drop your own; Surco squares and embeds it

groups.title: Analyze quality
items: Draws the spectrum and catches MP3s disguised as lossless before your set
Colour read-out: LUFS, true peak, dynamics, balance and noise
Detects BPM and key from the audio itself, one click to apply
Warns you if the track is already in your Apple Music library

groups.title: Organize & export
items: Send to Apple Music with tags and cover written into the library —even on WAV, which lands blank by hand— and keep the copy in sync as you edit
Export the whole queue as a rekordbox or Traktor collection
Name with {artist} - {title} templates and build subfolders
Import whole folders and preview any track before converting

shortcuts.title: Fly without touching the mouse
shortcuts.lede: A command palette and shortcuts for the whole flow — add, tag, analyze and process track after track without leaving the keyboard, and remap any of them.
items: Add files
Process track
Process all
Play / pause
Navigate tracks
Search Discogs

speed.title: From minutes to seconds per track
speed.lede: By hand you jump between a converter, the browser with Discogs, a tag editor, a spectrum analyzer and Apple Music — four or five apps per track. Surco folds that whole flow into one button.

manualSteps.label: Look up the info on Discogs

speed.manualCaption: jumping between tools · repeating on every track
bullets: lossless AIFF, WAV or FLAC, or MP3
speed.footnote: * Manual flow shown as an example; steps and tools vary from person to person. Estimated times.

steps.title: Drop your tracks
steps.body: Drag your WAV, FLAC, AIFF or MP3. Surco reads the tags and cover art on the fly.

steps.title: Pick the release
steps.body: Search Discogs and apply artist, album, year, genre and cover art in one click.

steps.title: Export and you're done
steps.body: Convert to AIFF, WAV, FLAC or MP3, check the spectrum and send the track to Apple Music.

pricing.title: Free, forever
pricing.lede: Surco is free and complete — no account, no subscription, no limits. If it saves you hours, you can support its development.

footer.tagline: Convert, tag and organize your DJ tracks — ready to play in seconds.

install.title: Download and install
install.lede: Download it for your system or install it with Homebrew. Either way, it updates itself.

download.intel: Intel Mac (2020 or earlier)? Download it here
download.unavailable: The download isn't available yet — we're polishing the first version.

guide.metaTitle: How to use Surco — step-by-step guide
guide.metaDescription: The complete Surco guide: import tracks, tag from Discogs, match a whole release, analyze the spectrum, convert and export to Apple Music, rekordbox or Traktor.
guide.title: How to use Surco
guide.lede: From dropping your tracks to having them ready to play. This guide walks the whole flow, step by step — no rush, nothing skipped.
guide.outroLede: Download Surco and get your first crate of tracks ready in a couple of minutes.

sections.title: Install and open Surco
body: Download Surco for your system from the home page, or install it with Homebrew on macOS. The first time you open it, a wizard gets you set up in a minute: pick your default output format, the folder to save to and, if you like, paste your Discogs token.
No account, no subscription. Surco updates itself, so you'll always have the latest version without downloading again.
points: Available for macOS (Apple Silicon and Intel) and Windows.
The welcome wizard sets up format, output folder, genres and visible fields.
You can change any setting later in Settings (⌘ ,).
sections.shotCaption: The welcome wizard on first launch.

sections.title: Add your tracks
body: Drag files —or whole folders— onto the Surco window, or click “Add files” (⌘O) to pick them. You can also open an audio file with Surco from Finder or Explorer.
Surco reads each file's tags, duration and cover art on the fly and shows them in the list instantly. The same file is never added twice.
points: Formats: WAV, FLAC, AIFF, MP3, M4A, AAC, OGG, OPUS and more.
Drop whole folders: Surco walks them and adds every audio file it finds.
Tracks show up immediately; tags and cover art load in the background.
sections.shotCaption: Dragging and dropping tracks into the list.

sections.title: Get to know the track list
body: The left column is your work queue. Each row shows the title, artist, duration and a status dot telling you whether the track is untouched, processing, ready or errored. Once you analyze quality, a green (good) or amber (suspect) dot appears.
Press Space to preview the selected track in the floating player before converting. Drag the right edge to widen the list, or double-click it to fit the longest track name.
points: Quick filters up top: All, Unanalyzed, Suspect, Good, Unconverted and Auto-matched.
Click to select one track; ⌘ (or Ctrl) + click to add several, and Shift + click for a range.
The ✦ icon marks tracks Surco filled in automatically from Discogs.
sections.shotCaption: The track list with filters and statuses.

sections.title: Edit the metadata
body: Select a track and the editor on the right shows every field: title, artist, album, year, genre, label, catalog, BPM, key, grouping, comment and more. Edit anything and you'll get suggestions from your saved genres and groupings as one-click pills.

Up top you can give it a star rating and manage the cover art: drag to upload, export it, or move between the file's cover and the Discogs ones with the arrows. Track has a tidy filename but no tags? Click “Fill from filename” and Surco infers title and artist.
points: Under BPM and key you'll see the value Surco detects from the audio itself — in Camelot or musical notation, per Settings — one click from applied.
Only the fields you choose in Settings → Fields are shown.
Required fields turn red if you leave them empty.
The cover shows its real pixel dimensions so you can judge sharpness.

sections.title: Check the real quality
body: Open the quality section and Surco draws the track's spectrum: frequencies from bottom to top across the song. A true lossless file reaches ~22 kHz; an MP3 disguised as WAV cuts off sharply around 16 kHz and leaves a gap up high. A dashed line marks where it cuts.

Below it, a color-coded readout sums up the levels: integrated LUFS, true peak, dynamics, channel balance, DC offset and noise floor — green, amber or red at a glance. To analyze the whole queue at once, use the toolbar button or ⌘⇧A.

Each track wears its verdict as a badge right in the list, and you can filter by “Suspect”, “Good” or “Unanalyzed” to triage a freshly dropped folder. The check pays off before a gig: a cutoff you’d never hear on headphones shows up the moment the track plays on a big rig.
points: Green “good quality”: the sound reaches all the way up, nothing missing.
Amber “suspect”: a sharp early cutoff — likely a re-encoded fake.
You can turn off the spectrum or loudness in Settings → Editor if you don't use them.
sections.shotCaption: A track's spectrum and level readout.

sections.title: Tag from Discogs
body: In the editor, search the release by name or paste a Discogs release id directly and hit “Search” (or the / key). Surco lists the results with cover art, year and label; expand one to see its tracklist.

Click the matching track and Surco applies artist, album, year, genre, label, catalog and cover art in one go — the closest match is flagged “Suggested”. Search works with no setup; add your own Discogs token in Settings for a higher, private rate limit.
points: Search by text or paste a release id if you already know it.
With a token and the option on, Surco auto-matches tracks right after import.
Your cover is kept if you already have one; the Discogs cover stays as an alternative.
sections.shotCaption: Searching a release and picking the track on Discogs.

sections.title: Auto-match: tagging without lifting a finger
body: With your Discogs token and “Auto-match on import” enabled in Settings, every file you drop searches itself on Discogs in the background. Surco scores each candidate against the file’s title, duration, track number and artist, and only if a match is confident does it apply the metadata for you — artist, album, year, genre, label and cover, exactly as if you had picked it by hand.

If no candidate clears that bar, nothing is touched: the track is left for you, and opening its Discogs search shows the best one flagged “Suggested”, one click from confirmed. With the toggle off (the default) the same holds: Surco suggests, but never updates on its own.
points: It never steps on your work: already-matched tracks are skipped, your cover art is kept, and re-running it only fills the gaps.
It probes rows as they scroll into view, and your manual searches always go first, so it never drains the request limit.
Filter the list by “Auto-matched” to review what it did, or run it on demand with “Auto-match all tracks”.
sections.shotCaption: The best match, flagged “Suggested” in the Discogs search.

sections.title: Match a whole release
body: Imported an album or a multi-track EP? Select them all —⌘ + click one by one, or Shift + click for a range— and the editor switches to multi-track mode: the header reads “Editing N tracks” and any field you edit applies to the whole selection at once.

In the Discogs column, search the release just once. Surco loads it and builds a grid: your files on the left, a dropdown with the release's tracklist on the right. It auto-assigns each file to its song by duration —even when they all share the same name— and you reassign by hand any that missed. When it's right, hit “Apply to N” and tag them all at once.
points: “Editing N tracks” header: edit title, artist, album or genre once for all of them.
The grid pairs each file with a song from the tracklist; change any assignment from its dropdown.
Duplicates are allowed: two files can point to the same song if needed.
In multi-track mode the spectrum and filename are hidden, since they're per-track.
sections.shotCaption: Assigning each file to its song on the release and applying to all.

sections.title: Name and organize the files
body: In Settings → Filenames you build the name template with chips: click {artist}, {title}, {album}, {year}… and they're inserted at the cursor. A live preview shows the result. Use the / separator to create subfolders, e.g. {albumArtist}/{album}/{title}.
By default Surco keeps the original name. In the editor, the regenerate button applies your template to that track in one click, and the pencil opens a custom pattern just for it.
points: Chips for every field: title, artist, album, year, genre, track #…
The / separator creates subfolders on export.
Optional: trim whitespace and zero-pad track numbers (03 instead of 3).
sections.shotCaption: The filename template builder in Settings.

sections.title: Convert the track
body: The button at the bottom of the editor converts the selected track. The dropdown next to it picks the format: AIFF, WAV or FLAC lossless, or MP3 at 320 kbps. If the track is already in that format, Surco copies it as-is instantly, without re-encoding.

In Settings → Conversion you choose the destination: an output folder, your Apple Music library, both, or overwrite the original in place. If you want, turn on normalization (loudness or peak, with streaming, club or broadcast presets) to even out the volume. When it's done, “Show file” takes you to the result in Finder or Explorer.
points: Formats: AIFF, WAV, FLAC (lossless) and MP3 320 kbps.
Re-exporting to the same format keeps Traktor cue points and beatgrid.
Normalization is off by default; it re-encodes the audio, so it's optional.
sections.shotCaption: The convert button and format choice.

sections.title: Convert the whole crate
body: Once the queue is ready, “Convert all” (⌘⇧Enter) processes every convertible track at once and skips the ones already done. You'll see progress and, at the end, a summary of how many were converted, skipped or errored.

Heads up: convert all only converts and renames using the metadata they already have — it never reaches out to Discogs on its own. If you want fresh data first, run “Auto-match” (⌘⇧D) from the toolbar.
points: Processes the whole queue at once; cancel mid-way if you need to.
Uses current metadata only: it downloads nothing from Discogs in the batch.
sections.shotCaption: Converting the whole queue at once.

sections.title: Take it to your decks
body: Surco doesn't stop at converting: it sends tracks straight to your Apple Music library (on macOS) as you convert, or exports the whole queue as a rekordbox (.xml) or Traktor (.nml) collection from the export modal (⌘⇧E).
Tags and cover art travel inside the file, so your DJ software reads them with no extra steps.
points: To Apple Music in one click, during conversion.
Export rekordbox (.xml) and Traktor (.nml) collections.
Surco warns you if a track is already in your Apple Music library.
Edit a track later and Surco updates its Apple Music copy instead of duplicating it.

sections.title: Fly with the keyboard
body: Surco is built to run without a mouse. The command palette (⌘K) puts everything one search away, and almost every action has a shortcut: add files (⌘O), process track (⌘Enter), process all (⌘⇧Enter), analyze quality (⌘⇧A), auto-match (⌘⇧D), search Discogs (/) and navigate the list with the arrows.
Press ? any time to see the full list, and reassign any of them in Settings → Shortcuts.
points: ⌘K opens the command palette; ? shows every shortcut.
Space plays or pauses; the arrows move the selection.
Every shortcut is reassignable in Settings.
sections.shotCaption: The command palette and the shortcut list.

sections.title: Tune it to your taste
body: Settings (⌘ ,) holds everything you don't touch day to day: the theme (light, dark or automatic), your Discogs token and auto-matching, the default format and destination, the name template, which fields you see and which are required, the maximum cover size and the shortcuts.
Set it up once and Surco honors your choices on every track.
points: General, Conversion, Filenames, Editor, Fields, Artwork and Shortcuts.
Define recurring genres and groupings so they show up as suggestions.
Choose which fields to show and which to require, and reorder them to taste.
Point the settings folder at iCloud Drive or Dropbox to share your preferences across Macs; the Discogs token never leaves your machine.

changelog.metaTitle: Surco changelog — What's new in each release
changelog.metaDescription: What's new in each Surco release: Discogs auto-match, rekordbox and Traktor export, loudness analysis, multi-track editing and more.
changelog.title: What's new
changelog.lede: The highlights of every release, without the noise. The features that change how you prep your music, version by version.
changelog.outroTitle: Try it all in your next session
changelog.outroLede: Download Surco and prep your first crate in a couple of minutes.

releases.title: Sharper quality verdict and smoother crate work
items: The quality verdict has a keener eye: a genuine but dull master is no longer flagged “Review” (good when there's no codec cut), and fake hi-res is now caught — files that claim 48/96 kHz but were upsampled from 44.1 kHz.
Auto-match now sweeps the whole crate, prioritizing the tracks on screen, and stops the moment you turn it off.
Editor sections you collapse stay collapsed as you move between tracks, and aren't analyzed until you open them — quicker when reviewing a big crate.
Drag several selected tracks out of Surco at once.
Drag an image straight from your browser as the cover.
The toolbar is reorganized into groups (edit · analyze · convert and export) so every action is easy to find.
The cover viewer steps through every image on the track: the one embedded in the file and the ones from the Discogs release.
Surco starts up faster and scrolls more smoothly through big crates, and the first play sounds instantly.

releases.title: Waveform player and sharper Discogs matching
items: The player now shows the track's waveform: click or drag on it to scrub, with the time floating on top.
Continuous playback: when a track ends, the next one in the list plays. Toggle it with the ∞ button in the player.
Much sharper Discogs matching: it ignores file-name noise (320, labels, “feat.”…), is accent-insensitive, understands more name formats, and retries by catalog number or title alone when needed.
With auto-match on, the track you select is matched on its own — no need to press the button on each one.
Dropping files that are already in the list now skips them and tells you, instead of doing nothing.
Tagging and “Add to Apple Music” are now available for WAV files too.
Adjust the player volume by scrolling over it; the level floats next to the time on hover.
Files whose highs were regenerated by an enhancer are now flagged “Reprocessed” instead of “Bad quality”, so a full-but-faked spectrum doesn't mislead.
Copy a track's generated file name — from your naming pattern — to the clipboard.

releases.title: Copy artwork and tags, drag tracks out
items: Drag a track from the list straight into any external app — Spek, Finder, your player — to check it instantly, no exporting first.
Copy a track's artwork and paste it onto another: from the button on the cover itself or with ⌘C/⌘V, through the system clipboard (to and from other apps too).
Copy a track's full metadata and paste it onto another from the right-click menu — perfect for sharing a release's details across its tracks.
Loudness normalization now actually reaches loud targets: the club preset no longer lands short — it applies the exact gain to target and a true-peak limiter holds the ceiling.

releases.title: Much more reliable spectrum analysis
items: The quality verdict has been rebuilt: dark masters and legitimate 320 kbps MP3s are no longer flagged as fakes for their natural high-end taper.
Grading is now absolute, the way a DJ reads it: reaching ~20 kHz is full quality (320 / lossless), 18–19.5 kHz is suspicious and below 18 kHz is bad — whatever the file's sample rate.
Surco now spots highs regenerated by audio “enhancers”: a track artificially glossed over a low-bitrate source no longer passes as good.
Soft encoder cutoffs (~160–192 kbps) that used to slip through as good quality are now caught and placed where they really are.
Reconstructed-highs detection: files “enhanced” from low-bitrate sources (SBR/enhancers) no longer pass as good quality even when their spectrum reaches the top.

releases.title: Apple Music, always in sync
items: Apple Music sync: re-converting an edited track updates its library copy — tags and artwork — instead of duplicating it.
New “Update in Apple Music” and “Show in Apple Music” actions in the editor; if you deleted the library copy, Surco imports it again.
Click the artwork to view it full size, at its real resolution — embedded art is pulled from the original file, not the thumbnail.
The field menu now also transforms text: Title Case, lowercase or UPPERCASE in one click.
While a track plays, the Dock icon animates with a sound wave.
Editing several tracks at once now shows only the fields you picked in Settings, just like editing one.

releases.title: Built for big crates
items: Your edits never get lost: typing while Surco is still reading a slow file, or while auto-match is querying Discogs, always keeps what you typed.
Much lighter with big crates: covers are kept as thumbnails (converted files still get the full-resolution art) and memory stops growing over long sessions.
Browsing the list feels smoother: BPM, key and loudness analyses wait until you rest on a track, and the one you're looking at always jumps ahead of the ones you left behind.
Every editor field can insert another field's value (artist into composer, title into comment…) from its own menu.
An amber dot marks tracks edited after converting, so you can see at a glance what still needs an update.
The Discogs search remembers what you typed on each track, shows a loading skeleton, and can be retried when it fails.
Names with a leading track number ("104. Artist - Title") now import with the right artist and title.
Adding several tracks to Apple Music shows its progress and can be cancelled; and when saving settings or trashing a file fails, Surco says so instead of failing silently.
Fixed: tracks whose cover was the file's own embedded art converted and landed in Apple Music with no artwork.
The player shows a loading indicator when audio takes a moment to arrive (for example, from a network drive).

releases.title: Richer metadata and quality in three colors
items: New metadata fields: composer, ISRC, mix, original year and a compilation flag. The composer fills itself from the Discogs writing credits.
The quality dot now grades three levels — green, amber and red — so a low-bitrate MP3 dressed up as lossless stands out, with an indicator while the spectrum is analyzed.
A button to clear all of a track's metadata, next to fill-from-filename.
Each list row shows its source format (WAV, FLAC, MP3…) so what still needs converting is obvious at a glance.
Start over in the context menu rebuilds a track from its original file.
The Apple Music check also looks up the Discogs-suggested title, catching library copies saved under the canonical name.

releases.title: BPM, key detection and free forever
items: BPM and key detection: Surco analyzes every track and suggests the tempo and key as one-click chips, in the key notation you prefer.
Surco is now free forever: licensing and limits are gone. If it saves you hours, you can support it with a donation from the Stats tab.
New conversion destination that overwrites the original file, with a confirmation before a batch.
Text search and sorting in the track list, with album-art thumbnails on every row.
The settings folder is configurable: point it at iCloud Drive or Dropbox to share your preferences across Macs.
A shortcut to switch the interface language between English and Spanish on the fly.
A slim progress bar in the header for long background sweeps.

releases.title: A better first run and accessibility throughout
items: The welcome wizard now sets up your output folder, visible and required fields, and teaches you to read the spectrum with a live preview.
New “Apple Music only” destination: the track lands in your library without leaving a copy in the output folder.
Full accessibility pass: the whole app works from the keyboard, plays well with screen readers and meets WCAG AA contrast.
Regenerate a file name in one click using the pattern from Settings.
Open audio files straight from Finder’s “Open With” menu.
New blue spectrogram palette that’s easier to read.

releases.title: Discogs auto-match and rekordbox & Traktor export
items: Auto-match: Surco looks your tracks up on Discogs and applies only high-confidence matches, on import or with a toolbar sweep.
Export your collection as a rekordbox (XML) or Traktor (NML) library.
Batch quality triage: analyze the whole list at once, badge every track with its verdict and filter the suspect rips.
Customizable keyboard shortcuts with clash detection and native menus that update themselves.
Per-track properties panel (codec, bit depth, bitrate, tag formats) and a rename dialog built from metadata chips.
Redesigned icons and tooltips across the app.

releases.title: Star ratings and cover control
items: Rate your tracks with stars; they’re written as tags Traktor, Windows Media Player and foobar2000 understand.
Pick the cover from every image the Discogs release has.
Artwork resolution is always visible and low-quality covers get flagged with a colored dot.
New Discogs release id field in the editor.

releases.title: Loudness and normalization
items: Loudness analysis (LUFS) and optional normalization that preserves your Traktor cues.
Right-click context menu on tracks and search inside the list.
Cancel a batch conversion mid-run.
Configurable default genres applied with one click.
Optionally send the original file to the Trash after converting.

releases.title: Multi-track editing and full albums
items: Multi-selection with bulk editing of shared fields.
Match a whole Discogs release against your files, pairing each track by duration.
Find & replace across every track’s tags, with regular expressions.
Fill tags from the file name in one click.
M4A/AAC, Opus and Ogg Vorbis now accepted as input formats.
The naming pattern supports subfolders to organize the output.

releases.title: Stability
items: More robust importing: hidden files and macOS leftovers are skipped when dropping folders.
Better tag reading on files with embedded pictures.

releases.title: First public release
items: Convert to AIFF, WAV, FLAC or MP3 by dragging your files in.
Discogs tagging with the most likely match suggested.
Spectrum analysis to catch fake lossless before you play out.
Send tracks straight to Apple Music, artwork included.
Template file names, a command palette, track preview and automatic updates.
macOS installs (Intel and Apple Silicon) plus Homebrew.
